perturbation.original_text: During the Promotion Period, iVillage traffic shall be audited by a third party traffic auditor listed on Exhibit A and iVillage shall provide FMM with relevant reports on a biweekly basis.
perturbation.changed_text: During the Promotion Period, iVillage traffic may be audited by a third party traffic auditor, potentially one listed on Exhibit A, and iVillage will endeavor to provide FMM with reports periodically.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces several ambiguities. 'May be audited' makes the audit discretionary rather than mandatory. 'Potentially one listed on Exhibit A' suggests the auditor might not be a recognized one. 'Endeavor to provide' weakens the obligation to provide reports, and 'periodically' lacks a specific timeframe, making it difficult to enforce regular reporting.
perturbation.law_citation: 15 U.S. Code § 7262 - Establishment, maintenance, and enforcement of standards and procedures for accounting treatment and financial reporting of corporate transactions and other matters
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text undermines the spirit and intent of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act, which emphasizes the importance of accurate and reliable financial reporting, including advertisement metrics that monetize traffic, by allowing for discretionary audits and vague reporting schedules. The act requires strict internal controls and audit procedures, which are compromised by the ambiguity introduced in the contract language.

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates a third-party audit of iVillage traffic during the promotion period, ensuring regular reporting to FMM every two weeks. The changed text introduces ambiguity and weakens these obligations by making the audit discretionary ('may be audited'), questioning the auditor's qualifications ('potentially one listed on Exhibit A'), and making the reporting less reliable ('endeavor to provide' and 'periodically'). The Sarbanes-Oxley Act, as explained by the LLM, emphasizes accurate and reliable financial reporting and strict internal controls. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is a 'Page Not Found' error, Scraped Law Snippet 1 from 15 USC 7262, discusses 'Management assessment of internal controls' and requires rules prescribing each annual report to contain an internal control report. Although this snippet is not directly about advertisement metrics it is about internal control. The shift from mandatory, regular audits to discretionary, potentially less qualified audits and vague reporting undermines the intent of ensuring reliable financial reporting, which the LLM explains goes against Sarbanes-Oxley.

perturbation.original_text: FMC shall have prior approval over all iVillage uses of any, Ford Mark, as defined below. In the event that any of the Ford-branded links and/or mentions set forth in this Section 2.F. are, in FMC's reasonable judgment, materially injurious to FMC. FMC shall provide written notice of such offense to iVillage. iVillage shall then have one (1) business day in which to cure said offense.
perturbation.changed_text: FMC shall have general input on iVillage's uses of any Ford Mark. In the event that any of the Ford-branded links and/or mentions set forth in this Section 2.F. are, in FMC's general perspective, potentially injurious to FMC. FMC may communicate concerns to iVillage. iVillage shall then address said concerns in a timely manner.
perturbation.explanation: The change from 'prior approval' to 'general input' significantly weakens FMC's control over its brand. Replacing 'reasonable judgment' with 'general perspective' introduces subjectivity. 'Materially injurious' becomes 'potentially injurious,' lowering the threshold for action. 'Provide written notice of such offense' becomes 'may communicate concerns,' making the notification discretionary. Finally, 'one (1) business day in which to cure said offense' becomes 'address said concerns in a timely manner,' removing the specific deadline and introducing ambiguity.
perturbation.law_citation: 15 U.S. Code § 1114 - Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text potentially contradicts the Lanham Act, which protects trademark owners from unauthorized use of their marks that are likely to cause confusion or deceive consumers. By weakening FMC's control over its brand and introducing ambiguity in the enforcement process, the modified text makes it more difficult for FMC to prevent infringement and protect its trademark rights, as timely action is required to prevent the brand from damage, and this clause gives iVillage all the time needed to remedy the issue.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 15 U.S. Code § 1114 - Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement by printers and publishers | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 1114 - Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement by printers and publishers U.S. Code Notes prev | next (1) Any person who shall, without the consent of the registrant— (a) use in commerce any reproduction, counterfeit , copy, or colorable imitation of a registered mark in connection with the sale, offering for sale, distribution, or advertising of any goods or services on or in connection with which such use is likely to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive; or (b) reproduce, counterfeit , copy, or colorably imitate a registered mark and apply such reproduction, counterfeit, copy, or colorable imitation to labels, signs, prints, packages, wrappers, receptacles or advertisements intended to be used in commerce upon or in connection with the sale, offering for sale, distribution, or advertising of goods or services on or in connection with which such use is likely to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive, shall be liable in a civil action by the registrant for the remedies hereinafter provided. Under subsection (b) hereof, the registrant shall not be entitled to recover profits or damages unless the acts have been committed with knowledge that such imitation is intended to be used to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive. As used in this paragraph, the term “ any person ” includes the United States, all agencies and instrumentalities thereof, and all individuals, firms, corporations, or other persons acting for the United States and with the authorization and consent of the United States, and any State, any instrumentality of a State, and any officer or employee of a State or instrumentality of a State acting in his or her official capacity. Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement by printers and publishers (1) Any person who shall, without the consent of the registrant- (a) use in commerce any reproduction, counterfeit, copy, or colorable imitation of a registered mark in connection with the sale, offering for sale, distribution, or advertising of any g
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens FMC's control over its brand, potentially contradicting the Lanham Act, which aims to protect trademark owners from unauthorized use that could cause confusion. The original text provided FMC with 'prior approval' and a specific process for addressing issues. The changed text replaces this with 'general input' and a vague 'timely manner' for addressing concerns. This ambiguity and reduced control directly undermines FMC's ability to prevent infringement, which is a key aspect of trademark protection as supported by the law snippets, specifically 15 U.S.C. § 1114, which addresses remedies for infringement.

perturbation.original_text: iVillage agrees to indemnify, defend and hold harmless FMM and its client Ford Motor Company and their respective parents, subsidiaries, affiliates, successors and assigns from any and all third party losses, liabilities, damages, actions, claims, expenses and costs (including reasonable attorneys' fees) which result or arise out of or in connection with the breach of this 5 Agreement by iVillage or which result or arise out of or in connection with any material supplied by iVillage pursuant to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: iVillage will consider offering indemnity to FMM and its client Ford Motor Company and their respective parents, subsidiaries, affiliates, successors and assigns from certain third party losses, liabilities, damages, actions, claims, expenses and costs (potentially including attorneys' fees) which arguably result from the direct breach of this Agreement by iVillage or directly arise from material negligently supplied by iVillage pursuant to this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The change weakens the indemnification clause by replacing 'agrees to indemnify, defend and hold harmless' with 'will consider offering indemnity', making the protection discretionary. 'Any and all' becomes 'certain,' limiting the scope of coverage. 'Reasonable attorneys' fees' becomes 'potentially including attorneys' fees,' adding uncertainty. The link is weakened to 'arguably result from the direct breach' and 'directly arise from material negligently supplied' adding additional limitations and ambiguity to the claim.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly contradicting it, the weakening of the indemnification clause combined with the 'negligently supplied' clause may create issues under New York General Obligations Law § 5-322.1. This law pertains to agreements exempting owners and contractors from liability for negligence. The original clause provided a clear indemnification. The modified clause is significantly weaker, and introduces questions on whether iVillage can be held responsible for damages even when iVillage may have been negligent, which this law regulates.

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-322.1 Agreements exempting owners and contractors from liability for negligence void and unenforceable; certain cases General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 3 § 5-322.1. Agreements exempting owners and contractors from liability for negligence void and unenforceable; certain cases. 1. A covenant, promise, agreement or understanding in, or in connection with or collateral to a contract or agreement relative to the construction, alteration, repair or maintenance of a building, structure, appurtenances and appliances including moving, demolition and excavating connected therewith, purporting to indemnify or hold harmless the promisee against liability for damage arising out of bodily injury to persons or damage to property contributed to, caused by or resulting from the negligence of the promisee, his agents or employees, or indemnitee, whether such negligence be in whole or in part, is against public policy and is void and unenforceable; provided that this section shall not affect the validity of any insurance contract, workers' compensation agreement or other agreement issued by an admitted insurer. This subdivision shall not preclude a promisee requiring indemnification for damages arising out of bodily injury to persons or damage to property caused by or resulting from the negligence of a party other than the promisee, whether or not the promisor is partially negligent. 2. A covenant, promise, agreement or understanding in, or in connection with or collateral to a contract or agreement relative to the construction, alteration, repair or maintenance of a building, structure, appurtenances and appliances including moving, demolition and excavating connected therewith, purporting to condition a subcontractor's or materialman's right to file a claim and/or commence an action on a payment bond on exhaustion of another legal remedy is against public policy and is void and unenforceable; provided that this subdivision shall not affect the validity of any insu
perturbation.justification: The modification significantly weakens the indemnification obligation, potentially conflicting with New York General Obligations Law § 5-322.1. The original text provides a clear agreement to indemnify, defend, and hold harmless. The changed text replaces this with 'will consider offering indemnity,' making the protection discretionary. The original text also specifies 'any and all' losses are covered. The changed text limits the scope of coverage to 'certain' losses, and modifies 'reasonable attorneys' fees' to 'potentially including attorneys' fees,' adding uncertainty. This weakening, coupled with the introduction of 'negligently supplied' clause, creates ambiguity about iVillage's responsibility for damages even in cases where negligence is involved, which is the primary target of the law. NY General Obligations Law § 5-322.1 states that agreements exempting owners and contractors from liability for negligence are void and unenforceable, directly contradicting the new language used.
